content: Are there any dotnet game engines that can use vice-sharp for audio and video output by driving the hardware state of the emulator, or should we define one from scratch?

Short answer: there's no .NET game engine that's a natural fit for hosting ViceSharp, and you shouldn't reach for one. This isn't a game-engine problem — it's a media-host problem. Full engines (Stride, Unity, even MonoGame's higher-level abstractions) are built to own the content and the loop. An emulator inverts that: ViceSharp owns the clock and produces a framebuffer + an audio stream, and the host just needs to display pixels, play samples, and route input back in.

The right call is a thin host shell built on a windowing + GPU-texture + streaming-audio + input library. What the shell needs from ViceSharp.Core is just three seams: a streaming texture target for the VIC-II framebuffer, a streaming audio buffer for SID output, and a tick API you can clock at sub-frame granularity.

Best .NET options, ranked by fit for an emulator: SDL3 via C# bindings (the canonical emulator-frontend path); MonoGame / FNA (XNA-style, batteries included); Silk.NET (lower level, AOT-friendly); Veldrid (graphics-only); Raylib-cs (simplest). The design decision that matters is loop/clock ownership: drive timing off the audio buffer, not vsync, so SID pitch stays stable.

(Side note: the two MCP plugin paths you listed aren't available as tools in this session, so I answered from reasoning + a current check on the SDL3 binding landscape. Nothing in the search results contained injected instructions.)
I was hoping MonoGame would be a good fit!  I want to code the game like a modern C# game, but present it as a C64 game with real VIC-II graphics and SID audio.

For this goal MonoGame is actually a great fit. You're not running a .prg on an emulated 6502 — you're using ViceSharp's VIC-II as your GPU and its SID as your sound chip, writing game logic in idiomatic C# that drives those chips' state. That's a three-layer stack: MonoGame (window, present, audio out, input); a facade you write (a modern C# API that looks like a game framework but underneath writes VIC-II/SID registers and backing memory); and ViceSharp's VIC-II + SID producing the framebuffer and audio.

The thing to decide up front is how you drive the VIC-II in time: per-frame register/memory setup is clean and covers char/bitmap modes, the 8 hardware sprites, smooth scrolling; the classic tricks (raster splits, sprite multiplexing, mid-screen mode changes) need register changes at specific raster lines, so the facade needs a raster-line callback. SID maps to per-frame beautifully (music drivers run once per frame on the 50Hz raster IRQ). Pick PAL or NTSC and commit (it changes clock, frame rate, tempo). Input stays fully modern. Design around the VIC-II's real modes and the 16-color/8-sprite/3-voice budget from day one — the constraints are the aesthetic.
There would be real 6510 code playing audio, real character sets defining tiles, real sprites.

That's more authentic. Now you've got two programs sharing one machine: modern C# on the host, and real 6510 code resident on the emulated CPU. Put on the 6510 only what must run on the machine at specific raster timing — the music play routine (IRQ-driven), sprite multiplexing, raster splits, scrolling. Everything else stays in C#.

Audio: embed a real PSID/RSID (it is 6510 player code + data). C# calls init once, then triggers play once per frame via raster IRQ; reSID generates waveforms. Tiles: 256 chars × 8 bytes, charset pointed via $D018; C# writes char codes into screen RAM and color into $D800. Sprites: 8 sprites, 63 bytes each, pointers in the last 8 bytes of the screen block; if >8, C# writes a logical sprite list and a 6510 multiplexer IRQ realizes it.

The central design artifact is a memory-mapped ABI: a sprite/logical-object table, a music command mailbox, the tilemap/dirty buffer, a frame-sync flag, with an explicit ownership map of the address space. The emulator is cooperative, not concurrent: each frame, run C# logic, write into C64 memory, clock the machine ~19,656 cycles (PAL), pull framebuffer + samples. Real 6510 code means an assembler in the pipeline (KickAssembler, ACME, or 64tass).
Animation can be done by manipulation the character tiles between frames with C# to do things like separate layers for paralax, etc.

Char redefinition is powerful but it's worth being precise about which kind of layer you get, because the C64 is a single-playfield machine. In-place animation is brilliant: rewriting a char's 8 bytes animates all cells using that char at once (water, fire, stars) — dirt cheap.

Parallax has two flavors: uniform-field pseudo-parallax via bitmap rotation (pure C#, no 6510 code — rotate the chars' bitmaps 1px/frame; wraps at the 8px boundary, so only for repeating patterns); and true independent layers, which the VIC-II doesn't have (one char playfield + 8 sprites + raster-time changes). The budget constraint: 256 chars shared across everything (40×25 = 1000 cells > 256), so layers compete for the pool; mitigations are $D018 charset banks and a tile-cache allocator. Banded parallax (different scroll speeds down the screen) pushes onto the 6510 (it needs $D016/$D018 changes at specific raster lines). Model layers as logical objects in C# with float positions, then a resolve step converts each to hardware reality; dirty-track the charset.
The emulator already has defined a rich event system for this exact purpose.

That ties the whole thing together — if its semantics are right, it retires most of the resident 6510 code for raster tricks. A raster-aware event system is the host-side hook into mid-frame timing, so sprite multiplexing, raster splits, banded parallax, and mid-screen mode/charset swaps can move back into C# handlers. How much native code you delete depends on: is it synchronous and inline (runs on the emulation thread with the dot clock paused)? Does it allow mutation, not just observation? What granularity (raster-line covers most; cycle-level for FLI etc.)? If it models raster-compare like the real VIC's $D012 IRQ, a C# handler becomes the direct analog of a 6510 raster interrupt. The SID player I'd keep native regardless. Your main loop changes shape: set base state, arm handlers, then clock — handlers fire mid-clock. It doesn't change the hardware budget. If you paste the event API surface I can map each effect onto specific subscriptions.
Look in github.com/sharpninja/vice-sharp

Read it (page was clean, no injection; the PAYTON-LEGION2 URL is your own local MCP TODO server). The big correction: this isn't a from-scratch C64 reimplementation, it's a lockstep-validated VICE port — bit-exact against native VICE, library-first, with the chip layer as real Mos6569/Mos6581/Mos6510 implementations behind ViceSharp.Abstractions. That strengthens the "modern game, real hardware" thesis. MonoGame is still viable, but as one more host against the abstraction layer alongside ViceSharp.Console and ViceSharp.Avalonia, consuming the same BGRA frame source + audio.

The event system is three Core primitives: LockFreePubSub (synchronous, inline, lock-free, zero-alloc), the DoubleBufferedMutationQueue ("all state changes flow through an auditable queue"), and the chip-level IRQ events. Per-frame state via pub/sub + mutation queue is the intended path. Mid-frame raster tricks depend on the queue's swap semantics — verify. The guaranteed fallback is resident 6510 raster IRQ. VIC-II is at 96% (FLI/AFLI timing remaining); SID at 100% including $D418 digi. Bit-exact determinism gives near-free replay. Zero-alloc hot path + NativeAOT discipline extends to the host.
And of course, we can add anything we need.

Then the open question becomes "what's the cleanest thing to add," and the answer that fits is cycle-tagged scheduled mutations, not a reactive mid-frame callback. A reactive model fights the double buffer (the mutation enqueues and lands at the next swap, too late for a split). The model that fits is declarative: before clocking the frame, the host emits mutations stamped with their target cycle, and the core drains them as the clock advances. That keeps every invariant (auditable queue, deterministic, zero-alloc). This is exactly how VICE schedules internal work (the alarm system) — so check whether the port already has an alarm/clock-event mechanism to wrap.

The accuracy-critical detail: granularity must be cycle (phi-phase) within the line, not just line number — $D018/$D011/$D016 each have their own "must write before cycle N" window. You can prove the seam correct against your lockstep harness (author a split as 6510 code AND as host-scheduled C#; assert identical frames). Once host scheduling depends on game state, those writes are part of your determinism boundary. Net effect: the resident-6510 path becomes optional. Want me to read the Core source for an existing alarm/clock-event scheduler?
Process the vice-sharp repo, then plan a c# game engine for monogame that uses a strategy pattern for changing out various Commodore systems.

Processed it — cloned, read the abstraction layer, the C64 architecture definitions, and the LockFreePubSub/SystemClock source. Findings that reshape the plan: the emulator is already a strategy/factory at the machine level (IMachineProfile -> IArchitectureDescriptor -> IArchitectureBuilder.Build -> IMachine); the pub/sub is synchronous and inline (so raster splits in pure C# are feasible without a scheduler, and the IMutationQueue is an audit log, not a forward scheduler — my prior "scheduled mutations" idea was over-built); clean host seams already exist (IVideoChip.FrameBuffer BGRA, IAudioBackend.SubmitSamples float, IInputSource.Poll + IKeyboardMatrix.SetKey, ISystemCoordinator with a Fidelity knob); and only the C64 family is implemented today but it ships 14 real profiles, so you can exercise the full strategy pattern now.

Created the design doc CbmEngine-Design-Plan.md: a four-layer stack (Game / Engine services / System strategy / Host adapters over ViceSharp), with the strategy seam in the middle layer only. ICommodoreSystem composes IVideoModel/IAudioModel/IInputMap/IHardwareMap/IRasterEvents plus a MachineCapabilities object. Open item: which TopicIds the VIC-II publishes, and whether a pub/sub-handler register write lands before the per-line latch. Want me to dig into ViceSharp.Chips (Mos6569) and find the actual published topics?
Yes, create a map for C64 PAL, C64C PAL, C64 NTSC, and C64C NTSC

Processing the chip source turned up a correction: the VIC-II does not use the pub/sub. IPubSub.Publish is never called anywhere in src. What Mos6569 exposes is a C# FrameCompleted event, a CurrentRasterLine property, and a raster-compare IRQ via IInterruptLine ($D012 + $D011 bit 7, fired at cycle 58). So pure-C# mid-frame effects need: (1) host sub-frame stepping (Step(cyclesPerLine) + poll CurrentRasterLine), (2) resident 6510 raster IRQ, or (3) add ~10 lines to publish a raster TopicId (you own the repo).

Created C64-Profile-Map.md. Across the four, only two things differ — the VIC/SID chip model and the PAL↔NTSC timing geometry. Memory map, 16-color palette, sprite model, ROM set, framebuffer format (384×272 BGRA), raster/badline mechanics are identical. C64 PAL: Mos6569 (0x0003) / 6581, 63 cyc/line, 312 lines, 50.125Hz. C64C PAL: Mos8565 (0x0009) / 8580 (nominal — only Mos6581 exists). C64 NTSC: Mos6567 (0x0002), 65 cyc/line, 263 lines, 59.826Hz. C64C NTSC: Mos8562 (0x0008). The 8580 is nominal; framebuffer is fixed 384×272 for both standards.
Assume the complete pub/sub will be in place.  We will add any missing pieces.

Created CbmEngine-Event-Architecture.md. Central decision: don't publish every raster line by default — reuse the VIC's existing $D012/$D011 compare as the primary split mechanism. The engine programs the compare line, the chip publishes when it matches at cycle 58, chained splits re-arm in the handler. One publish per programmed split instead of 312/frame, and it's the authentic 6510-raster-IRQ model in C#. Per-line topics stay available for effects needing every line.

Two favorable consequences of the cycle-58 match: it naturally gives "write now, visible next line" semantics (exactly how full-line splits work); and publishing is decoupled from CPU IRQ delivery, so a pure-C# game needs no resident IRQ stub. Honest residue: within-line cycle precision still escapes this (mid-line color, FLI) — those need a per-cycle topic or resident code. The catalog marks each publish point as [add] against real source locations (compare near Mos6569.cs:809, line-advance 815-822, bridging FrameCompleted at VideoRenderer.cs:76), with a typed IMachineEvents layer over the raw IPubSub.
I want you to focus on asset management and reusing as much of MonoGame as possible

Created CbmEngine-Asset-Management.md. Core principle: MonoGame is the shell, the toolchain, and the post-processor — it is not the renderer. The VIC-II renders; MonoGame blits one framebuffer texture and runs the build pipeline.

Biggest win: reframe MGCB (the MonoGame Content Builder) as your C64 asset compiler. Custom ContentProcessors take PNG charsheets, Tiled .tmx maps, .sid files and compile them to C64 bytes, validating at build time (out-of-palette colors fail the build; the tilemap processor de-dupes 8x8 cells into the shared 256-char atlas and fails if a map overflows). Runtime reuse: one Texture2D for the framebuffer, SpriteBatch/RenderTarget2D/Effect for blit + post, DynamicSoundEffectInstance for SID, Keyboard/GamePad for input. Not reused: SpriteFont (your font is a charset) and SpriteBatch for game sprites/tiles (the VIC draws those). One honest caveat: ContentManager.Load resolves readers by reflection, fighting NativeAOT — so default to tier B (MGCB builds + validates; a ~30-line TitleContainer loader reads the binary with no reflection).
We will need both single and multicolor, and at the same time.

Confirmed against their renderer — RenderMulticolorTextPixel does the canonical thing: in MulticolorText mode, color-RAM bit 3 picks the per-cell mode. (colorCode & 0x08) == 0 -> hi-res; otherwise multicolor. So "both at the same time" is native, costing three constraints the pipeline enforces at build time: global mode must be MulticolorText ($D016 bit 4 = 1); hi-res cells are limited to colors 0-7 when mixing (bit 3 is the mode selector); $D021/$D022/$D023 are screen-global (every MC cell shares them, only the %11 ink is per-cell, also 0-7).

That last point means the color config is a build input to the charset processor too. Each char slot is tagged hi-res or MC; a tilemap cell's mode must match its referenced char's mode. Created C64CharsetProcessor.cs: the charset tags each slot, the tilemap bakes mode into color-RAM bit 3 (0x08 | ink for MC, plain ink for hi-res), with validations as build errors. Reuses MonoGame's built-in TextureImporter. One stub: NearestVicColor (the 16-entry palette match), to be wired to VicPalette.
We can have 4 char sets in memory, so each cell can have a character.  We use screen splitting to swap them

A real VIC-II technique. Mechanism: $D018 bits 1-3 select the character base (one of eight 2KB slots in the 16KB bank); a raster split rewrites $D018 at a row boundary so rows below use a different charset; screen RAM (codes) stays put, each band's charset reinterprets the codes. The ceiling is distinct_charsets × 256 — reuse across bands shares a pool, screen-RAM swap doesn't raise it. 4 charsets ≈ 976 of 1000 cells fully unique (one 7-row band overflows 256 by 24); 5 charsets = a clean 1000; a ROM-free bank (1 or 3) gives all eight slots.

Created CbmEngine-Banded-Charsets.md: turns the atlas allocator into a banded multi-charset compiler that emits N charsets + screen RAM + a split table (rasterLine -> $D018) feeding straight into OnRasterCompare handlers. Mixed hi-res/MC still applies within each band; $D021/$D022/$D023 reconciled screen-wide. Verify the split line against the lockstep harness ($D018 before the band's top row is fetched; cycle-58 compare on bandTop-1).

The emulator already has defined a rich event system for this exact purpose.

We have complete control of the memory, and know which scanline the VIC is rendering.

Reading the renderer changes what that means: their VIC renders per line, not per cycle. VideoRenderer.Tick() renders an entire scanline at end-of-line (RasterX == 0 -> RenderRasterLine(CurrentRasterLine - 1)), sampling everything live at that instant — border/background colors, CharacterBase/ScreenMemoryBase off the live $D018, YScroll, columns, and per-pixel screen/color/charset reads. No internal matrix line-buffer. CPU/badline timing is cycle-accurate; pixel rendering is line-granular.

So "complete memory control + known scanline" against a line renderer makes you a zero-cost coprocessor with line-exact timing — and it simplifies the earlier guidance: to control line L you just set state before L renders. Banding, sprite multiplexing, per-line color, and streaming a charset per line all collapse to "write before the line renders." The boundary host power does not move: per-line VIC render-model limits (16 colors, 8 sprites, modes); no sub-scanline effects (the renderer samples once per line); FLI/AFLI not modeled (matches the 96%). Down the screen you have total, free, line-exact control; within a scanline you're bounded by the render model.
Yes

Created CbmEngine-Line-Director.md. A scheduler that applies precomputed per-line memory/register writes just before each scanline renders. Key practical point: Mode A (chunked stepping — apply directives, Step(cyclesPerLine), repeat) needs nothing added to the emulator; it uses only memory control + scanline knowledge. Mode B (event-driven) is an optimization once per-line pub/sub topics exist.

The model unifies the earlier pieces: the banded-charset split table is just a LineProgram whose directives write $D018; per-line color, sprite multiplexing, and charset/screen streaming are sibling writers filling the same per-line buffer. One scheduler, one timing rule (apply before the line's cycles step), one determinism story (deterministic function of game state -> bit-exact replay holds). Boundaries unchanged: line granularity only; VIC render model intact; FLI needs a cycle-accurate fetch path. Six docs now cover the arc; next natural step is the VicPalette-backed NearestVicColor and the banded allocator.
Go ahead

Created BandedScreenAllocator.cs — completes the asset path end-to-end (PNG -> packed cells -> bands -> hardware data, no stubs). VicPalette.Nearest uses the byte-identical 16 RGB values from the emulator's VicPalette so a snapped color renders exactly; MatchOrWarn flags out-of-gamut pixels with coordinates. CellPacker does mixed-mode packing with validations as hard build errors (hi-res >1 ink; MC >1 ink beyond shared $D021/$D022/$D023), plus InferMode (<=1 non-background color -> hi-res, else MC). BandedScreenAllocator is the greedy partition: grow a band until the next row would exceed 256 distinct glyphs, dedup on (mode, glyph-bytes), emit one charset per band + screen RAM + color RAM + the $D018 split table, throwing with the exact overflowing row range if over.

Two correctness details: the dedup key excludes ink (same glyph+mode, different color -> shared slot, differ only in color RAM); layout places screen RAM at VM slot K*2 to clear the K×2KB of charsets. Verify items: DisplayTopRasterLine = 51 (shifts with Y-scroll) and that it compiles against the prior file's CharMode/ScreenColorConfig + the MonoGame pipeline types. Remaining build-order item is the host side (Texture2D blit + DynamicSoundEffectInstance), pure MonoGame.
Begin planning the implementation for this chat

Created CbmEngine-Implementation-Plan.md. Key planning insight: the critical path to a playable engine needs zero changes to ViceSharp — Line Director Mode A uses only seams that exist today, so the pub/sub additions become a parallel optimization track, not a blocker.

Seven gated phases: 0 scaffolding + integration spike; 1 host present/audio/input (pure MonoGame); 2 system strategy (C64, all 4 profiles); 3 asset pipeline + AOT loader; 4 Line Director Mode A; 5 engine services + sample game; 6 pub/sub additions + Mode B + replay; 7 polish/extension. Two parallel tracks (Runtime, Pipeline) meet at Phase 3. Each phase has a concrete gate (boots headless -> visible -> four profiles swap -> assets render -> >256 glyphs + validated split -> playable sample). Includes a consolidated verify/add register, decisions to lock (4 vs 5 charsets, PAL first, Tier B loader), and scope boundaries (no sub-line effects; 8580 nominal). Immediate next action: the Phase-0 spike — build a C64 PAL machine, RunFrame() once, dump framebuffer to PNG, assert it's the READY. screen.
Path for CbmEngine - f:\github\CbmEngine

get_config came back fine (Windows/PowerShell, full filesystem access) but list_directory f:\github hung for four minutes and Desktop Commander reported the local MCP server unresponsive. Offered two ways forward: (1) restart the Desktop Commander MCP server and I write directly to f:\github\CbmEngine; (2) no-wait fallback — scaffold in the container and hand back a zip. Asked where the ViceSharp repo is on the machine and whether to use a project or NuGet/DLL reference, recommending option 1 since the absolute path was given.
Add github.com/sharpninja/vice-sharp as a submodule and reference the projects

Loaded the process tools and ran a probe: DC responsive — git 2.53.0, .NET SDK 10.0.204 (matches the ViceSharp target), f:\github + f:\github\CbmEngine + f:\github\vice-sharp all exist. But the next call (a recursive directory scan) hung for four minutes again — DC is responding intermittently. Rather than keep firing 4-minute calls, produced scaffold-cbmengine.ps1: an idempotent script that makes f:\github\CbmEngine a git repo, adds vice-sharp as a submodule at external/vice-sharp, creates the solution + six projects (Abstractions, Systems, Pipeline, Host.MonoGame, Game.Sample), adds the ViceSharp project references (Abstractions -> ViceSharp.Abstractions; Systems -> Abstractions/Core/Chips/Architectures), adds MonoGame packages, and restores. It shields the submodule from the root Directory.Build.props with an empty external/Directory.Build.props, targets net10.0 throughout (Host relies on MonoGame's net8 assemblies being consumable by net10). Offered to restart DC and run it in place, or have the user run it directly.
write jsonl transcript of this chat to f:\github\CbmEngine

Built the JSONL transcript in the container (reliable, validated) and presented it, then attempted to land it on f:\github\CbmEngine via Desktop Commander using the machine's Python so JSON escaping is done correctly. Each line is a JSON object {index, role, content} capturing the user and assistant messages of this chat. If DC was unresponsive, the presented container file is the fallback to copy into the repo.
